title: Emden, IL — Drafted
description: Harold E. Krusemark was drafted from his home in Emden, Illinois and inducted into the U.S. Army at Peoria. This was the beginning of a decade of military service across two wars and three theaters.
quote: I was inducted in the U.S. Army on the 2nd of October in 1942 at Peoria, Illinois…

title: Camp Grant — Active Duty
transport_label: By rail to Camp Grant, Illinois
description: Reported for active duty at Camp Grant, near Rockford, Illinois.
quote: …and reported for active duty on October 16th, 1942 at Camp Grant, Illinois.

title: Fort Bragg — FA Instructor
transport_label: By rail to Fort Bragg, North Carolina
description: Completed Basic Training and remained as a Field Artillery instructor at Fort Bragg.
quote: My Basic Training was at Fort Bragg, North Carolina where I stayed on assignment as an instructor in the Field Artillery.

title: Fort Sill — 2nd Lt, FA
transport_label: By rail to Fort Sill, Oklahoma
description: Completed Officer Candidate School and received a commission as 2nd Lieutenant in Field Artillery.
quote: I received a 2nd Lieutenant's commission in Field Artillery at Fort Sill, Oklahoma.

title: Camp Rucker — Motor Officer
transport_label: By rail to Camp Rucker, Alabama
description: Served as Wheeled Vehicle Motor Officer in the Service Battery of the 161st Field Artillery, 35th Division.
quote: I was then assigned to serve as a Wheeled Vehicle Motor Officer in the Service Battery of the 161st Field Artillery of the 35th Division at Camp Rucker, Alabama.
near_miss: The 35th Division later deployed to Normandy (Omaha Beach, July 1944), fought through France, and into the Battle of the Bulge. Harold was transferred out before deployment. Men he trained alongside fought in Normandy and beyond.

title: Tennessee Winter Maneuvers
transport_label: By motor convoy to Tennessee maneuver area
description: Participated in Tennessee winter maneuvers near Nashville with the 35th Division.
quote: The Division participated in the Tennessee winter maneuvers just outside of Nashville…

title: Camp Butner — 35th Division
transport_label: By motor convoy to Camp Butner, North Carolina
description: Moved with the 35th Division to Camp Butner, North Carolina.
quote: …and then moved to Camp Butner, North Carolina.

title: Fort Bragg — 100th Division
unit: Battery B, 375th Field Artillery Battalion, 100th Division
transport_label: By rail to Fort Bragg, North Carolina
description: Reassigned as Assistant Executive Officer in Battery B, 375th Field Artillery Battalion, 100th Division.
quote: Being reassigned as an Assistant Executive Officer in Battery B of the 375th Field Artillery Battalion of the 100th Division, again I was back to Fort Bragg, North Carolina.
near_miss: The 100th Division deployed to France in October 1944 and fought 185 consecutive days in the Vosges Mountains. During training, the division shed over 14,636 enlisted men and 1,400 officers to other units. Harold was one of those transferred out before the division shipped overseas.

title: Fort Benning — Infantry Retraining
transport_label: By rail to Fort Benning, Georgia
description: Retrained as an infantry officer. With massive infantry casualties in Europe, many artillery officers were reassigned to the infantry.
quote: …many artillery officers were reassigned to the infantry. I was one of those and went to Fort Benning, Georgia for infantry retraining.
near_miss: Harold was retrained from artillery to infantry specifically because so many infantry officers were being killed in Europe. The Battle of the Bulge alone caused approximately 89,000 American casualties.

title: Camp Cooke — Co M, 303rd Infantry
unit: Company M, 303rd Infantry Regiment, 97th Infantry Division
transport_label: By rail to Camp Cooke, California
description: Joined Company M, 303rd Infantry, 97th Division as a platoon officer and prepared for overseas deployment. Later served as train commander moving materiel to the New York Port of Embarkation.
quote: My next assignment took me to Camp Cooke, California as a platoon officer in Company M of the 303rd Infantry in the 97th Division. I served as train commander along with three enlisted guards in the caboose of a freight train across the United States from California to New York.

title: Camp Kilmer — Staging for Europe
transport_label: By rail across the United States — Harold in the caboose as train commander
description: The division moved from Camp Cooke to Camp Kilmer, New Jersey — the staging area for the New York Port of Embarkation. Harold served as train commander for the freight cars carrying the division's equipment across the country, riding in the caboose with three enlisted guards. At Camp Kilmer the regiment underwent final training and reequipping. On February 16, all personnel were restricted to camp in preparation for overseas movement.
quote: While there we prepared for overseas shipment by loading all of our materiel onto rail cars bound for the New York port of embarkation. I served as train commander along with three enlisted guards in the caboose of a freight train across the United States from California to New York.

title: USS Monticello — Atlantic Crossing
transport_label: By ferry to Staten Island; boarded USS Monticello
description: Awakened at 0200 on February 17, Company M boarded the USS Monticello at Staten Island. L and M Companies were assigned to shipboard guard duty for the crossing. The transport got underway after midnight on February 19. Two days later the seas turned rough — many soldiers seasick, guards unable to stand at their posts. On the night of February 27, depth charges were dropped somewhere in the Atlantic. On March 1 the French coast came into view with many ships anchored off Le Havre. On March 3 the company disembarked at 0500, loaded into horse vans, and rode 40 miles toward Dieppe in one of the coldest days Lt. Gatner said he had ever known. They breakfasted on K rations at 0800 and arrived at Camp Lucky Strike near Fécamp at 0930.
quote: The company disembarked at about 0500 and loaded in horse vans for a 40-mile trip in the direction of Dieppe. It was one of the coldest days I have ever known. About 0800 we stopped and breakfasted on K rations. Arrived at Camp Lucky Strike at 0930 — somewhere near Fecamp.

title: Le Havre — Arrival in Europe
transport_label: By ship — USS Monticello — New York to Le Havre, France
description: Landed at Le Havre, France, and staged at Camp Lucky Strike. The 97th Division prepared to relieve the 78th Division on line.
quote: We arrived in LaHarve, France and staged to relieve the 78th Division on line.
quote_secondary: We landed on March 3rd, 1945 at Le Havre, France… Then we moved inland to a Camp 'Lucky Strike' where we remained over 3 weeks…

title: Wockrath — First Billets in Germany
transport_label: By motor convoy through France, Belgium, and Holland into Germany
description: Entered Germany and billeted in old farmhouses in Wockrath while preparing to cross the Rhine. Passed through Julich and Aachen, both almost totally destroyed.
quote: Our first billets were in the small town of Wockrath, somewhere north of Düsseldorf…

title: Cologne & Rhine Crossing
transport_label: By motor convoy south from Wockrath through Cologne to the Rhine
description: The convoy moved south from Wockrath through Cologne — a city Harold named as one of the key objectives of the Ruhr campaign. Cologne had been captured by the 1st Infantry Division on March 5–7, just weeks earlier, and was 95% destroyed by years of Allied bombing. Nearly 40,000 tons of bombs had fallen on the city. The Cathedral of Cologne — one of the great Gothic cathedrals of Europe, 600 years in the building — stood almost completely intact in the middle of total ruin, a landmark that Allied pilots had been ordered not to bomb because it served as a navigation beacon. Harold passed through the rubble and continued south to the Rhine, crossing on a pontoon bridge near Bonn. The regiment spent the night there before pushing south toward the Sieg River.
quote: Our mission was to close the Ruhr Pocket by taking the key cities of Cologne, Solengen, and Dusseldorf in the Central Europe Campaign and the many, many small villages in between.

title: Sieg River — Mortar Positions
transport_label: By motor convoy, crossed the Rhine on a pontoon bridge near Bonn
description: Crossed the Rhine and deployed mortar sections along the Sieg River, relieving 78th Division units. Conducted reconnaissance and supported the 2nd Battalion's assault crossing with heavy mortar fire.
quote: One morning, Lt. Krusemark, who commanded my first mortar section, and I, went out on a reconnaissance and found a church whose steeple was still intact and would make an excellent observation post.
quote_secondary: Krusemark and I were standing in a town square… A large shard fell at our feet… if that shell fragment had hit either of us, it would have sliced us in half.

title: Hilden — 88mm Battery Destroyed
transport_label: Advancing on foot and by truck through the Ruhr Pocket
description: Rapid advance through the Ruhr Pocket. At Hilden on April 16, Lt. Krusemark's mortar section destroyed a combined 88mm anti-aircraft and searchlight battery, resulting in 61 prisoners taken. The 303rd then entered Düsseldorf, helping complete the elimination of the Ruhr Pocket. This action earned Harold the Bronze Star Medal.
quote: At Hilden, with their mortars set up on the lawn of the Battalion C.P., Lt. Krusemark's section destroyed a combined 88mm anti-aircraft and searchlight battery. As a result, about 61 prisoners were taken.
quote_secondary: Second Lieutenant Krusemark's exemplary performance of duty in active ground combat was in keeping with the finest traditions of military service and reflects great credit upon himself, the 303d Infantry Regiment, and the Army of the United States.

title: Düsseldorf — Gestapo HQ, Stahlhaus
transport_label: By truck convoy north, accompanied by a platoon of tanks
description: The 3rd Battalion took Düsseldorf on April 17, accompanied by a platoon of tanks. The first objective — Gestapo Headquarters — was secured without a shot fired. Company M was billeted in the basement of the Stahlhaus, a large steel-framed office building in the city center. The Ruhr Pocket had collapsed. The 97th Division had captured 21,791 prisoners and cleared nearly 1,000 square miles in a 10-day campaign. After four days in Düsseldorf, the battalion loaded back onto trucks and headed east toward Czechoslovakia.
quote: Accompanied by a platoon of tanks and all troops riding, the 3d Battalion took Düsseldorf. The first objective, the Gestapo Headquarters was taken without a shot being fired. Company M was billeted in the basement of the Stahlhaus, a large office building.

title: Flossenbürg — Concentration Camp Liberation
transport_label: By truck convoy eastward through Bavaria
description: During the 97th Division's rapid advance toward Czechoslovakia, a military police patrol from the 303rd Infantry Regiment — Harold's own regiment — was among the first American units to reach the Flossenbürg concentration camp. Flossenbürg was a major Nazi concentration camp where over 30,000 prisoners died, including pastor Dietrich Bonhoeffer, executed just two weeks earlier. The MP patrol found thousands of emaciated survivors, mass graves, and evidence of atrocities. The regiment had already liberated the Hoffnungstal POW camp on April 14 and encountered columns of displaced persons and death-march survivors throughout the advance.

title: Czechoslovakia — Final Advance
transport_label: By truck convoy east from Düsseldorf toward the Czech border
description: The battalion drove east from Düsseldorf, halting briefly at Hof before crossing the Czech border into Asch on April 21. The 97th Division pushed 28 miles in 30 hours across a 25-mile front, taking more than 20,000 prisoners. The regiment advanced through Tirschkenreuth, Mähring, Chodov, and Kasta Tepla — using a monastery as a prisoner-of-war enclosure. Harold's own account notes he was ten miles from Plzeň when the war ended. On May 7 at 2300, the ceasefire order arrived. The 97th was one of the few American divisions still attacking. On May 8, a lone German fighter strafed the 3rd Battalion command post.
quote: After the surrender of Dusseldorf, we were on our way to liberate Czechoslovakia and was only ten miles from Pilsen when the war in Germany ended.

title: Bamberg — Medals & Redeployment Orders
transport_label: Withdrawn by truck to assembly area near Bamberg, Germany
description: After being relieved by the 16th Armored Division, the regiment withdrew to an assembly area at Sceschlitz near Bamberg. General Halsey's command post was established at Scheßlitz Castle. On May 25, a regimental review was held and medals were formally awarded — including Harold's Bronze Star for the Hilden action. The regiment received orders for Pacific redeployment. On May 30, the battalion boarded freight trains westward through Schweinfurt, Würzburg, Aschaffenburg, Frankfurt, Mainz, and Saarbrücken, back toward France.
quote: Friday, May 17 — Moved to Sceschlitz near Bamberg. Camped in a wooded area. Saturday, May 25 — Regimental review and the awarding of medals.

title: Freight Train Through Ruined Germany
transport_label: By freight train westward across Germany toward France
description: Left Bamberg on freight trains at 0800 on May 30. The route passed through Schweinfurt, Würzburg, Aschaffenburg, and Frankfurt — cities reduced to rubble by Allied strategic bombing. Through Mainz, Bad Kreuznach, and Saarbrücken on May 31, crossing back into France at Longuyons on June 1. The journey offered hours of unbroken views of what the combined air campaign and ground war had done to Germany's industrial heartland. Two months earlier, these men had been fighting through this same country. Now they were riding home through its ruins.
quote: Thursday, May 30 — Left Bamberg at 0800 on freight trains for our return to France. Passed through Schweinfurt, Wurzberg, Aschaffenberg, and Frankfurt. Friday, May 31 — Passed through Mainz, Bad Kreuznach, Bad Munster, and Saarbrucken. June 1 — Longuyons, France.

title: Paris — Short Pass
transport_label: By freight train west through Germany and France to Amiens area
description: The regiment staged at Camp Old Gold near Amiens while awaiting transport home. Paris, liberated less than a year earlier, was 130 kilometers to the south — well within reach of a weekend pass. Harold's family has a photograph of him in Paris from this period, among the Eiffel Tower and the grand boulevards still crowded with Allied soldiers.

title: Camp Old Gold — Amiens, Waiting to Sail
transport_label: By freight train from Bamberg through France to Amiens
description: The regiment arrived at Camp Old Gold near Amiens on June 2 — a redeployment staging area in the French countryside. On June 14, they boarded ship at Le Havre. Two days later, on June 16, the transport sailed for New York. The men had been told they would receive 30 days' leave and then report to Fort Bragg — before being sent to Seattle to sail for Japan.
quote: Sunday, June 2 — Amiens and Camp Old Gold. Friday, June 14 — Left Old Gold at 0530 and went aboard the transport at 0930. Sunday, June 16 — Sailed from Le Havre at 1400.

title: Return to New York
transport_label: By ship — Le Havre (June 16) to New York Harbor
description: Arrived in New York Harbor at 0800 on June 24. Debarked at 1500, took a ferry to Jersey City, then by train to Camp Shanks. The following day they moved to Fort Dix. On June 26, leave orders were issued and the regiment dispersed for 30 days of rest and recuperation — with orders to reassemble at Fort Bragg, North Carolina.
quote: Monday, June 24 — Arrived in New York harbor at 0800. Debarked at 1500 and took the ferry to the Jersey shore and from there Camp Shanks by train. Wednesday, June 26 — Leave orders issued and the regiment dispersed for 30 days of R & R.

title: Camp Shanks — Back on American Soil
transport_label: By ferry from New York Harbor to the Jersey shore; by train to Camp Shanks
description: After debarking in New York Harbor, the regiment ferried to the Jersey shore and rode by train to Camp Shanks in Orangeburg, New York. The following day they moved to Fort Dix, New Jersey. On June 26, leave orders were issued — the regiment dispersed for 30 days of rest and recuperation, with orders to reassemble at Fort Bragg, North Carolina.
quote: Tuesday, June 25 — Left Camp Shanks for Fort Dix. Wednesday, June 26 — Leave orders issued and the regiment dispersed for 30 days of R & R, to reassemble at Fort Bragg, North Carolina.

title: Emden, IL — Home Leave
transport_label: By rail to Emden, Illinois
description: Thirty days of rest and recuperation at home in Emden, Illinois, after returning from Europe.
quote: Within a couple weeks we were on our way back to New York. I enjoyed a two weeks leave…

title: Fort Bragg — Reassembly for Pacific
transport_label: By rail to Fort Bragg, North Carolina
description: After 30 days of leave, the division reassembled at Fort Bragg — the same base where Harold had done his Basic Training three years earlier. The men knew they were being readied for the Pacific. The planned invasion of Japan, Operation Downfall, would require millions of soldiers. The 97th Division had been designated as a reserve force for Operation Coronet, the invasion of Honshu south of Kyoto.
quote: July — Division reassembled at Fort Bragg.

title: Fort Lewis — Reequipping for Japan
transport_label: By rail to Fort Lewis, Washington
description: The division moved to Fort Lewis, Washington to reequip for Pacific operations. On August 6, the atomic bomb fell on Hiroshima. On August 9, Nagasaki. Japan agreed to surrender terms on August 14 — while the 97th was still preparing to sail. Despite the surrender, the division continued to Seattle and boarded ships, now bound for occupation duty rather than invasion.
quote: August — Division moved to Fort Lewis, Washington, for reequipping prior to shipment to the Pacific.

title: Seattle — Sailing for Japan
transport_label: By rail — Fort Bragg to Fort Lewis to Seattle
description: Reported to Seattle as the 97th Division prepared to sail for Japan. The division had been designated as a reserve force for Operation Coronet — the planned invasion of Honshu, south of Kyoto. Japan had already surrendered on August 14, but the division still proceeded.
quote: …was ordered to report to Seattle, Washington where the 97th was preparing to go to Japan. The war with Japan ended while we were on the way but the Division still landed and served as occupation forces.
near_miss: Operation Downfall was the planned two-phase invasion of Japan: Operation Olympic (Nov 1945, invasion of Kyushu) and Operation Coronet (Spring 1946, invasion of Honshu). The 97th Infantry Division was designated as a reserve force for Operation Coronet. The operation would have been seven times the scale of Normandy, with estimated hundreds of thousands of American casualties. The atomic bombs on Hiroshima (Aug 6) and Nagasaki (Aug 9) ended the war. Harold and the 97th sailed from Seattle on August 30 aboard the USS General John Pope — as occupation forces instead of an invasion force.

title: Yokohama — Arrival in Japan
transport_label: By ship — USS General John Pope — Seattle via Ulithi and Leyte to Yokohama
description: Arrived in Tokyo Bay / Yokohama as part of the occupation forces. When the 97th arrived, soldiers observed three rows of trenches dug along the shoreline — the defenses Japanese forces had prepared to oppose the planned invasion. Division veterans who saw these defenses believed many more lives would have been lost had the invasion taken place.
quote: The war with Japan ended while we were on the way but the Division still landed and served as occupation forces.

title: Kumagaya & Ogashima — Occupation Duty
transport_label: By electric train to Kumagaya area, north of Tokyo
description: Occupation duty north of Tokyo. Conducted motorized reconnaissance patrols through Japanese towns. Later moved to Ogashima airfield. Took an orientation flight in U.S. Army transport planes.
quote: Capt. Peterson, Lts. Foster, Krusemark, and I went on a motorized reconnaissance patrol. We went through Haniu, Osi, Kumagaya, and a number of smaller towns.
quote_secondary: Two U.S. Army transport planes… landed. The pilots took Capt. Peterson, Lts. Foster and Krusemark, Sgt. Fiebrich, and five other enlisted men up for a ride.

title: Camp McCoy — WWII Separation
transport_label: By ship — Japan to United States
description: Returned to the United States and separated from the service at Camp McCoy, Wisconsin, retaining a Reserve Commission.
quote: I returned to the States the latter part of May in 1946 and was separated from the service at Camp McCoy, Wisconsin on the 18th June with a Reserve Commission.

title: Emden, IL — Home, Teaching
transport_label: By rail to Emden, Illinois
description: Returned home to Emden, Illinois and worked as a teacher. Harold retained his Reserve Commission, which would bring him back to service when the Korean War began.

title: Fort Sheridan — Korean War Recall
description: Recalled to active duty for the Korean War at Fort Sheridan, Illinois. On June 5th, departed for the Far East Command.
quote: Having kept a Reserve Commission I was recalled to active duty at Fort Sheridan, Illinois on May 15, 1951. On June 5th I was on the way to the Far East Command at Yokohama, Japan headed for Korea.

title: Chorwon Valley — Korea
unit: 4.2" Chemical Mortar Platoon, 15th Infantry Regiment, 3rd Infantry Division
transport_label: By ship — Fort Sheridan to Yokohama to Korea
description: Served as Fire Direction Center officer in the 4.2" Chemical Mortar Platoon, supporting infantry in the Chorwon Valley / Iron Triangle area. The 3rd Infantry Division held a sector of the Jamestown Line. By mid-1952, the war had settled into static positional warfare with constant mortar and artillery duels. Chinese forces steadily escalated bombardment through the summer of 1952. Harold experienced a major enemy mortar attack roughly a month before heading home.
quote: I was assigned to the 4.2" Chemical Mortar Platoon in the 15th Infantry Regiment of the 3rd Division as a fire direction center officer. We supported foot troops by firing white phosphorus shells prior to their advancement. We also set up defensive holding actions in the Chorwan Reservoir area.
near_miss: By summer 1952, Chinese forces were firing 45,000 rounds per month against the Eighth Army front, building toward the largest artillery volumes of the entire war. Harold experienced a major enemy mortar attack roughly a month before his rotation home.

title: Fort Sheridan — Final Separation
transport_label: By ship — Korea to United States
description: Returned to the United States and separated from active duty on September 17, 1952, ending a decade of military service across two wars and three theaters.
quote: I returned to the States the first part of September in 1952 and was separated from the service at Fort Sheridan, Illinois on September 17.

title: Emden, IL — Home for Good
transport_label: By rail to Emden, Illinois
description: Harold returned home to Emden, Illinois for good — a decade of service complete. Two wars, three theaters, a Bronze Star, and a journey that took him from central Illinois across Europe, to the shores of Japan, and to the hills of Korea.
